pros: Among the top five shoes in the world for out-of-the-box performance. Virtually no break-in period. One of the best smearing shoes the reviewer has worn. Crushes toe-hooks due to leather upper. Well rounded and adapts to different styles
cons: Build quality leaves something to be desired -- will disintegrate if brutalized. Wears quickly outdoors. No-Edge sole takes time to get used to. Less experienced climbers may burn through shoes quickly
fit_notes: Leather upper stretches approximately one full size. Available in low-volume model. Soft 1.1mm Laspoflex midsole with No-Edge sole design
best_for: Competition climbing, gym training for developing footwork, smearing-heavy routes

pros: One of the best board climbing shoes on the market. Edges hard, maintaining grip through shoe structure alone. Possibly the best heel in the La Sportiva range. Durable and does not delaminate. Climbs well on slab and in an overhang
cons: Not the best toe-hooking shoe. Stiffness naturally limits sensitivity. Hanging off the rubber is not the shoe's specialty. Break-in period longer than older model. At $210, La Sportiva's most expensive climbing shoe
fit_notes: Women's model fits the same as men's but with split sole for greater flexibility. Zero dead-space while offering precision and control
best_for: Low-angle slab, big wall, sport climbing on small edges, trad, crack, board/gym climbing

pros: The best edging shoe the reviewer has ever worn. Excels at almost all types of climbing. Great at smearing with rubber that holds up well. Feels like an extension of the foot. Can easily be the only pair you use
cons: Not as downturned as specialized bouldering shoes -- mediocre pocket performance. Toe hooking abilities are mediocre. Shoe width is snug, problematic for wide feet
fit_notes: Size 0.5-1 sizes below street shoe (US). Designed for low-volume heels -- high-volume heels may slip. Tight fit, unsuitable for wide feet. Stretches over first few weeks
best_for: Face climbing, edging-intensive routes, crack climbing, smearing. Not ideal for heavily overhanging boulder problems

summary: WeighMyRack highlights the Mantra as one of the lightest and most sensitive climbing shoes available, featuring La Sportiva's No-Edge Technology for a natural feel on volumes and smears. They note it is not designed for small edges on slab or mountain trad climbing.
pros: One of the lightest and most sensitive climbing shoes; No-Edge Technology for natural feel on volumes
cons: Not ideal for small edges on slab; Not a mountain trad shoe
best_for: Modern competition climbing, bouldering, indoor gym

summary: GearJunkie found the Mantra to be super sensitive and pliable, with a comfortable high-performance fit and excellent toe hooking capability. They noted the heel can be too voluminous for narrower feet and the minimal construction could mean lower durability.
pros: Super sensitive and pliable; Comfortable for a high-performance fit; Excellent toe hooking
cons: Heel can be too voluminous for narrow feet; Minimal construction may mean lower durability

pros: The best edging shoe on the market, hands down. Both versatile and high performance. Heel hooking was a dream with thick rubber patch. P3 technology retains shape over lifespan. Build quality is impressive
cons: Not a great shoe for toe hooking -- lacks thick rubber on toe box. Will not be comfortable for crack climbing, low-angle smearing, or all-day routes. Many people develop a bump on their big toe (Miura bump)
fit_notes: On the narrow side, will not work for wide feet. Painful break-in period. Sized half size down from trad shoes. Designed to be sized tightly for optimal performance. Quite painful on hot days with foot swelling
best_for: Steep boulders, technical sport climbs, vertical granite trad routes

pros: Stiff and downturned making it awesome at edging. Very comfortable given its aggressive build. First pair lasted two years with one resole. Great for pockets due to pointed toe box. Surprisingly good at smearing for an aggressive shoe
cons: Not very good at toe hooking -- small rubber patch and straps risk undoing. Stiffness compromises crack performance. Will never be as sensitive as some shoes. Strap stickiness degrades after two years
fit_notes: Size 1-1.5 sizes smaller than street shoe. Expect quite a bit of stretch -- difficult initially but easier after breaking in. Fits like a glove with skin-tight fit throughout shoe life
best_for: Bouldering, sport climbing, slab climbing. Not for crack climbing or very overhung problems

pros: Construction quality was the highest in the test. Downturn maintained over time. Great heel hooking stability with S-Heel. P3 system offers a stable edging platform. Fits wider feet well with snug fit for narrow heels
cons: Average toe hooking. Limited sensitivity -- P3 system and stiffness detract from tactile sensations. Stiffness interferes with twisting the shoe. Limited rubber coverage on heel
fit_notes: Wide toe box fits wide forefoot, accommodates narrow heel. Microfiber upper softened quickly with use. Reviewer downsized a full size from street shoe. Shoes stretched minimally
best_for: Slightly overhanging sport routes and boulders

pros: Super supple and very soft, much like predecessor Cobra. Perform incredibly in a gym. Very impressed by how sticky the rubber is
cons: Stretch up to a full size and are very soft. Must fit as tight as you possibly can -- they stretch even after first use
fit_notes: Fit as tight as you possibly can. Stretch significantly, even after first use. Sizing tight is essential due to stretching behavior
best_for: Gym training, crushing long-term boulder projects outside

pros: Precision on the most marginal of features -- you can feel the hold. Plenty of rubber on top for toe hooking. Come on and off quite easily. Pushed reviewer to climb with new confidence
cons: Not for everyone -- intended for lower volume feet specifically
fit_notes: Intended for lower volume feet. Unlined leather stretches to foot -- get them a little tight. Reviewer wears street 11.5, chose size 42 for pretty tight performance fit
best_for: Steep sport climbing, vertical to overhanging limestone, small holds and pockets

pros: Incredibly versatile -- performs on steep bouldering, techy sandstone, and steep terrain. Edges really well due to supportive construction. More sensitive than Solutions (thinner 0.8mm midsole)
cons: Not as sensitive as hoped for certain smeary moves. Toes protested on less steep terrain while breaking in
fit_notes: Stretched quite a bit, more than any other Sportiva. Reviewer typically wears 44.5-45 Sportiva but comfortable in 43.5. Medium width feet fit perfectly, narrow feet don't quite fill it out
best_for: Steep sport climbing, bouldering, all-round performer

pros: Soft construction and sticky rubber make it one of the most capable smearing shoes. Dramatic downturn retained shape after 5 months of heavy use. S-Heel provides locked-in feel during hooks
cons: Insecure on small edges and pockets -- soft build lacks support. Foot has to work harder, causing fatigue. XS Grip 2 rubber wears faster than XS Edge
fit_notes: Built on a wide last for an aggressive shoe. Sized same as Miura VS and TC Pro. Unlined toe box stretches during break-in, significant comfort increase over time
best_for: Steep bouldering, smearing, heel/toe hooking on overhanging rock

pros: One of the best shoes for all-round climbing. Great at edging on granite crystals. Narrow toe is amazing for thin cracks. Velcro strap positioned high gives lots of jamming space
cons: Not as good at standing on tiny edges as the La Sportiva Genius. Toeing into tiny pockets is tiring for the foot
fit_notes: Very tight when first strapped on, loosens up after a few sessions. Can then be worn for extended periods of time
best_for: Granite climbing, thin cracks, indoor training, short powerful limestone routes

pros: Easy to tighten with fast lacing and easy-to-grab loops. P3 system tension ensures aggressive shape is maintained. Arch support from midsole architecture
cons: Not super-versatile for outdoors. Uncomfortable for long routes. Tight fit becomes problematic in hot weather when feet swell
fit_notes: Extremely snug fitting that works well indoors but creates discomfort during extended outdoor use in warm conditions
best_for: Super-aggressive steep climbing and bouldering, competition climbing

pros: Very capable on both vertical and overhanging routes. Medium-soft build with half-length midsole is powerful on vertical edges yet flexible enough for steep pulling. Updated velcro closure boosts durability
cons: Tensioned heel is extremely uncomfortable, digs into the Achilles. Feet tire easily. Not versatile for crack, multi-pitch, or less-than-vertical climbing. Difficult to get on and off
fit_notes: Very uncomfortable out of the box. Synthetic/leather upper designed to stretch a bit -- size quite snugly. After a few sessions the shoe fits well. Women's has thinner 0.9mm midsole vs men's 1.1mm
best_for: Steeper-than-vertical climbing, 45-degree overhanging walls, toe and heel hooking

pros: Stand on dime edges with ease. Perhaps no better shoe for climbing pockets. Ultra-sticky Vibram XS Grip rubber on steep terrain. No significant rubber delamination after heavy use
cons: Nylon strap degrades rather quickly from abrasion. Pointy toe box less than ideal for sustained friction slabs. More fatiguing on long edgy pitches than a classic edging shoe. Heel cup is bulbous and insensitive
fit_notes: Reviewer sized quite small. Could have been better on slabs with half a size up. Tight fit is expected
best_for: Steep overhanging terrain, pockets, bouldering and route climbing

pros: An incredible redesign of an already incredible shoe. Best all-around climbing shoe in the world -- excels at bouldering, sport, crack, big walls, alpine. Durable enough to save money in the long run. Comfortable even when fit tight
cons: New ECO leather might hold stink more. New TC Pro reportedly stretches more than old one. Not ideal for 45-degree walls and bouldering caves. At ~8.7oz, not a lightweight shoe
fit_notes: Tight at first as leather stretches and molds to foot. Important to size appropriately. Comfortable sizing is possible for all-day use
best_for: Vertical and slabby terrain, all-day multi-pitch, trad climbing

pros: Precise edging with decreased foot fatigue. Transitions easily from face to crack to offwidth. Ankle protection. Built to last with full-length LaspFlex rand and stiff midsole
cons: Expensive. New models require longer break-in time. Eco leather feels rigid and holds foot odor more. Rand can still roll off after frequent crack climbing. Less toe sensitivity than sport/gym shoes
fit_notes: Best suited to medium-to-wide width foot. Stretch a decent amount with minimal wear. New model fits a bit larger than older model, slightly more narrow in the toe box. Break-in time longer than old model
best_for: Trad climbing, big wall, multi-pitch, crack climbing, technical face climbing

pros: Best smearing shoe the editor has ever used indoors. Best slab climbing shoe for indoor competition. An edging monster on the board. Heel marries stiffness of Solution heel. Vibram XS Grip 2 feels stickier due to aggressive downturn
cons: Heel tabs a little uncomfortable out of the box. Toe patch weaker than Solution on jugs -- will not do the work for you. Not ideal for thin little edges. Design places foot under some stress
fit_notes: Requires a tight size for greatest performance. Leather insole allows enough stretch to make tight sizing worth it. Lower volume model also available. Designed to crush after a few sessions of break-in
best_for: Indoor competition bouldering (slabs and overhangs), board climbing, overhung climbing requiring heel hooks
